answers:
**1. Where new provider calls should live, and whether the current code follows that pattern everywhere.**

New refund calls belong in `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py`. That module's own docstring says so directly: *"This module is the single place the chargekit provider client is built. All calls to the payment provider go through the accessors below."* (`gateway.py:3-4`), matching `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`. The correct shape would be a new `refund(...)` accessor next to the existing `charge()` (`gateway.py:11-13`) and `create_customer()` (`gateway.py:16-17`), built on the module-level `_client` (`gateway.py:8`). Callers (routes, workers) should import that accessor, never `chargekit` directly.

**No, the current code does not follow that pattern everywhere — and in fact `gateway.py` is currently dead code.** I grepped the whole project for any import of `app.billing.gateway` and found zero matches: nothing in the codebase actually calls `gateway.charge()` or `gateway.create_customer()`. Every real call site instantiates `chargekit.Client` on its own instead:

- `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:4` (`import chargekit`) and `:14` (`client = chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")`) inside `charge_invoice()`. This has been the case since the very first commit, `23d2f37` — the same commit that added `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` claiming the gateway was the sole entry point.
- `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py:2` (`import chargekit`) and `:9` (`chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY").charge(...)`) inside `run_dunning_pass()`. Added in commit `3a7daee` (MEDB-197).

The only place that matches the doc's stated exception is `medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py:6-9`, which imports solely `chargekit.errors` (the exception classes), exactly as `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:6-7` describes.

So there are **two undocumented violations** (`invoices.py`, `dunning.py`), and the module the doc says is mandatory is presently unused. If refund support is built by copying the existing precedent (a third inline `chargekit.Client(...)`), it would entrench the violation rather than fix it.

**2. `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` vs. actual code — invoice API response and payment integration.**

- **Payment integration** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`): claims every provider call goes through `gateway.py` except `errors.py`. As shown in (1), this is false: `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` both call `chargekit` directly, and `gateway.py` is never invoked at all. The doc names one exception; there are actually two additional, unlisted ones, and the "rule" itself isn't followed by any live code path.
- **Invoice API response** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:11-12`): claims the response contains *exactly* `id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status`. The actual `InvoiceOut` dataclass (`medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py:16-22`) has a sixth field, `external_ref`, populated from the provider's charge result and returned by `charge_invoice()` (`invoices.py:17-18`). This field was added in commit `c23da88` (MEDB-214, 2026-06-18), backed by `medibill/backend/migrations/0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql`, without any change to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in that commit (confirmed via `git show --stat c23da88`, which touches `invoices.py`, `webhooks.py`, `models/invoice.py`, and the migration file, but not `docs/`). The doc's "exactly" claim was accurate against the original schema (`migrations/0001_step1.sql`, 5 columns) but has been wrong since MEDB-214.

**3. What should happen to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when.**

Per the skill's standard — drift is a bug, and the contract must be corrected in the same commit as the code that invalidates it — this is a **stop-and-surface moment before writing any refund code**, not a cleanup to defer. Concretely:

- The doc needs to be corrected now, as its own explicitly-called-out change, before or atomically with the refund feature — not silently folded into the refund PR's diff. Refund support is exactly the kind of payment-integration change this doc governs, so building it on top of a contract that's already been false for weeks (since 2026-06-18) compounds the same failure a third time.
- For the payment-integration section, the team needs to make an explicit choice rather than have it improvised: either (a) fix `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` to route through `gateway.py`, restoring the truth of the "every call goes through gateway.py" rule, or (b) if there's a real reason those two need direct access, update the doc to name them as accepted exceptions. Given the skill's guidance that "an existing exception is not a license to add a new one elsewhere without the same scrutiny the original one presumably got," and that `gateway.py` is currently *unused* (not a case of one or two deliberate carve-outs alongside heavy legitimate use), option (a) looks like the right default — but that's a call for whoever owns this code, not one I should make unilaterally while read-only.
- The invoice API section needs `external_ref` added to the field list (or the field list needs to be re-derived from `InvoiceOut` directly — see Q4's logic, which applies here too).
- Whatever refund support adds — a new `gateway.refund()` call site, any new response field, any new webhook type — must land with its own doc update in the same commit, called out in the commit/PR summary, per the skill's core rule.

**4. The webhook event-type count claim.**

`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:16-17` states: *"The integration handles 4 webhook event types: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened."* The actual handler, `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py:7-20`, branches on **5** types: `payment_succeeded`, `payment_failed`, `payment_pending`, `refund_created`, `dispute_opened`. `payment_pending` was added in commit `c23da88` (MEDB-214) — the same commit that added `external_ref` — again with no accompanying change to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`.

This is precisely the failure mode the skill's "Don't cite a number: read the source" guidance warns about: a hardcoded count in prose that was correct when written and has since silently gone stale. What I'd change about how it's stated: drop the numeral ("4 webhook event types") and either list the types without asserting a total, or better, point at `app/api/webhooks.py`'s dispatch as the source of truth rather than duplicating the list in prose at all. A refund feature is likely to add at least one more event type (e.g., a `refund_failed`/`refund_succeeded` case), so a restated fixed count would just go stale a second time; a doc that names the file instead of a number can't drift the same way.
report:
Confirmed: within this repository, `medibill` is the sole project with `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and the only one integrating with a payment provider. I now have everything needed to write the report.

## Plan

The task asked me to review, read-only, how the backend that will gain refund support currently integrates with its payment provider, and to check whether `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` still matches that code. I first read the mandated skill (`architecture-contracts-as-law`) to understand the standard I should hold the doc to: contract docs are meant to be merge-blocking law, updated in the same commit as any code that changes what they govern, with drift treated as a bug. I then located the target project (this repo contains many unrelated small apps; only `medibill` has a `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and a payment integration), read every file touching the payment provider, cross-checked each of `ARCHITECTURE.md`'s three claims (gateway boundary, invoice API shape, webhook event types) against the actual code, and used git history to see exactly when and how the doc and code diverged.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/architecture-contracts-as-law/SKILL.md` in full (required by the task prompt). Key guidance applied below: contract docs are law, not documentation; drift is a bug; boundary modules should be the sole call site for a vendor SDK and this is machine-checkable via grep; schema/API changes must update the contract doc in the same commit; avoid citing counts that can silently drift — point at the source instead.
- `find . -maxdepth 3 …` — surveyed the repo root; it holds many unrelated small SaaS projects (cedarline, clinic-notes, deskly, medislot, etc.). Only `medibill` matches the task's description (has `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, a `backend/app/billing/` module, and webhook tests).
- `find ./medibill -type f -not -path '*/.git/*'` — full file listing of the target project.
- Read `medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` (17 lines) — the contract doc, three claims: payment-provider boundary, invoice API response shape, webhook event types.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py` — the intended/documented sole boundary module; defines `charge()` and `create_customer()` over a module-level `chargekit.Client`.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py` — the doc's one named exception; imports only `chargekit.errors`.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py` — the invoice charge route.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py` — `Invoice`/`InvoiceOut` dataclasses and `InvoiceOut.from_model`.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py` — the webhook intake route.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py` — the nightly retry worker.
- `Grep "chargekit" ./medibill` — enumerated every reference to the provider SDK across the whole project, to make sure no call site was missed (this is the exact "grep for the invariant" check the skill recommends).
- Read `medibill/README.md`, `medibill/tests/test_webhooks.py`, `medibill/frontend/src/App.jsx`, `medibill/infra/main.tf` — checked for any other integration surface or doc reference; frontend/infra are stubs, the only webhook test is a placeholder (`assert True`).
- `cat` all six files in `medibill/backend/migrations/` — confirmed the original invoice table schema (5 columns, migration `0001_step1.sql`) and a later migration `0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql` adding an `external_ref` column.
- `git log --oneline` inside `medibill` — 3 commits: `23d2f37` (MEDB-180, initial backend + architecture doc), `3a7daee` (MEDB-197, dunning worker), `c23da88` (MEDB-214, external payment ref).
- `git show --stat` on all three commits, then full `git show c23da88` and `git show 23d2f37 -- backend/app/api/invoices.py backend/app/billing/gateway.py docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` — established exactly which commit introduced each piece of drift, and confirmed `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` has never been touched since the initial commit.
- `Grep "billing\.gateway|from.*gateway import|import gateway"` and then a broader `Grep "gateway" -i` across `./medibill` — confirmed `gateway.py`'s functions are never imported or called anywhere in the codebase.
- `Grep "refund" -i` across `./medibill` — confirmed no outbound refund-issuing code exists yet; `refund_created` only appears as an inbound webhook case.
- `find . -iname "*chargekit*"` from the repo root — confirmed the `chargekit` SDK is an external dependency, not vendored in-repo.
- Final scoped check: `find . -iname "ARCHITECTURE.md"` and `grep -rl "chargekit" --include="*.py"` from the repo root — confirmed `medibill` is the only project in this repository with an architecture doc or a payment integration (an earlier, wider `cd ..` briefly left this repo into sibling sandbox directories that are not part of this project; the shell auto-reset to the correct working directory and this final check was re-run correctly scoped).

## Answers

**1. Where new provider calls should live, and whether the current code follows that pattern everywhere.**

New refund calls belong in `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py`. That module's own docstring says so directly: *"This module is the single place the chargekit provider client is built. All calls to the payment provider go through the accessors below."* (`gateway.py:3-4`), matching `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`. The correct shape would be a new `refund(...)` accessor next to the existing `charge()` (`gateway.py:11-13`) and `create_customer()` (`gateway.py:16-17`), built on the module-level `_client` (`gateway.py:8`). Callers (routes, workers) should import that accessor, never `chargekit` directly.

**No, the current code does not follow that pattern everywhere — and in fact `gateway.py` is currently dead code.** I grepped the whole project for any import of `app.billing.gateway` and found zero matches: nothing in the codebase actually calls `gateway.charge()` or `gateway.create_customer()`. Every real call site instantiates `chargekit.Client` on its own instead:

- `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:4` (`import chargekit`) and `:14` (`client = chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")`) inside `charge_invoice()`. This has been the case since the very first commit, `23d2f37` — the same commit that added `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` claiming the gateway was the sole entry point.
- `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py:2` (`import chargekit`) and `:9` (`chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY").charge(...)`) inside `run_dunning_pass()`. Added in commit `3a7daee` (MEDB-197).

The only place that matches the doc's stated exception is `medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py:6-9`, which imports solely `chargekit.errors` (the exception classes), exactly as `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:6-7` describes.

So there are **two undocumented violations** (`invoices.py`, `dunning.py`), and the module the doc says is mandatory is presently unused. If refund support is built by copying the existing precedent (a third inline `chargekit.Client(...)`), it would entrench the violation rather than fix it.

**2. `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` vs. actual code — invoice API response and payment integration.**

- **Payment integration** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`): claims every provider call goes through `gateway.py` except `errors.py`. As shown in (1), this is false: `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` both call `chargekit` directly, and `gateway.py` is never invoked at all. The doc names one exception; there are actually two additional, unlisted ones, and the "rule" itself isn't followed by any live code path.
- **Invoice API response** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:11-12`): claims the response contains *exactly* `id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status`. The actual `InvoiceOut` dataclass (`medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py:16-22`) has a sixth field, `external_ref`, populated from the provider's charge result and returned by `charge_invoice()` (`invoices.py:17-18`). This field was added in commit `c23da88` (MEDB-214, 2026-06-18), backed by `medibill/backend/migrations/0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql`, without any change to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in that commit (confirmed via `git show --stat c23da88`, which touches `invoices.py`, `webhooks.py`, `models/invoice.py`, and the migration file, but not `docs/`). The doc's "exactly" claim was accurate against the original schema (`migrations/0001_step1.sql`, 5 columns) but has been wrong since MEDB-214.

**3. What should happen to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when.**

Per the skill's standard — drift is a bug, and the contract must be corrected in the same commit as the code that invalidates it — this is a **stop-and-surface moment before writing any refund code**, not a cleanup to defer. Concretely:

- The doc needs to be corrected now, as its own explicitly-called-out change, before or atomically with the refund feature — not silently folded into the refund PR's diff. Refund support is exactly the kind of payment-integration change this doc governs, so building it on top of a contract that's already been false for weeks (since 2026-06-18) compounds the same failure a third time.
- For the payment-integration section, the team needs to make an explicit choice rather than have it improvised: either (a) fix `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` to route through `gateway.py`, restoring the truth of the "every call goes through gateway.py" rule, or (b) if there's a real reason those two need direct access, update the doc to name them as accepted exceptions. Given the skill's guidance that "an existing exception is not a license to add a new one elsewhere without the same scrutiny the original one presumably got," and that `gateway.py` is currently *unused* (not a case of one or two deliberate carve-outs alongside heavy legitimate use), option (a) looks like the right default — but that's a call for whoever owns this code, not one I should make unilaterally while read-only.
- The invoice API section needs `external_ref` added to the field list (or the field list needs to be re-derived from `InvoiceOut` directly — see Q4's logic, which applies here too).
- Whatever refund support adds — a new `gateway.refund()` call site, any new response field, any new webhook type — must land with its own doc update in the same commit, called out in the commit/PR summary, per the skill's core rule.

**4. The webhook event-type count claim.**

`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:16-17` states: *"The integration handles 4 webhook event types: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened."* The actual handler, `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py:7-20`, branches on **5** types: `payment_succeeded`, `payment_failed`, `payment_pending`, `refund_created`, `dispute_opened`. `payment_pending` was added in commit `c23da88` (MEDB-214) — the same commit that added `external_ref` — again with no accompanying change to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`.

This is precisely the failure mode the skill's "Don't cite a number: read the source" guidance warns about: a hardcoded count in prose that was correct when written and has since silently gone stale. What I'd change about how it's stated: drop the numeral ("4 webhook event types") and either list the types without asserting a total, or better, point at `app/api/webhooks.py`'s dispatch as the source of truth rather than duplicating the list in prose at all. A refund feature is likely to add at least one more event type (e.g., a `refund_failed`/`refund_succeeded` case), so a restated fixed count would just go stale a second time; a doc that names the file instead of a number can't drift the same way.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, in this order:

1. Raise the two `gateway.py` bypasses (`invoices.py`, `dunning.py`) and the two silent doc-vs-code gaps (`external_ref`, `payment_pending`) with whoever owns `medibill` *before* starting refund work, since the refund feature both depends on and would extend the payment-integration contract.
2. Get an explicit decision on `invoices.py`/`dunning.py`: refactor them to call `gateway.charge()` (making `gateway.py` actually load-bearing), or formally document them as exceptions — then make the code and `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` agree, in one commit.
3. Update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`'s invoice-API section to include `external_ref`, and its webhooks section to name `payment_pending` and drop the hardcoded count.
4. Add `refund()` to `gateway.py` (mirroring `charge()`/`create_customer()`), a route that calls only that accessor, and — if refunds arrive as webhooks too — extend `webhooks.py`'s dispatch, all landing together with the corresponding `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` update in the same commit.
5. Replace the placeholder in `tests/test_webhooks.py:1-2` with real tests asserting the actual set of handled event types, so this specific drift (doc says N, code does N+1) is caught mechanically next time rather than by manual doc review.
6. Consider a small grep-based check (the skill gives the exact pattern) run in CI: flag any `import chargekit` outside `app/billing/gateway.py` and `app/billing/errors.py`, so a third silent bypass can't reappear. (`medibill` currently has no CI workflow and no such guard script, unlike at least one sibling project in this repo that does have an equivalent check.)

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence, directly verified by reading the code and git history:
- The gateway-bypass finding (Q1/Q2): confirmed both by reading `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` line-by-line and by a repo-wide grep showing zero call sites for `gateway.charge`/`gateway.create_customer`. This is as solid as read-only inspection gets.
- The `external_ref` field and `payment_pending` gaps (Q2/Q4): confirmed by direct code inspection, cross-checked against the migration that added the column and the exact commit diff (`git show c23da88`) that added both changes without touching `docs/`.
- No existing refund-issuing code: confirmed by a repo-wide case-insensitive grep for "refund," which only found the inbound webhook branch, nothing that calls out to the provider.

Lower confidence / judgment calls, not facts:
- My Q3 recommendation to fix `invoices.py`/`dunning.py` to route through `gateway.py` (rather than document them as accepted exceptions) is my read of the skill's stated preference, not something the repository itself dictates — I flagged it as a decision for the code owner rather than asserting it as the only correct answer, per the skill's own instruction to treat an ambiguous contract as a stop-and-surface moment rather than something to improvise past.
- I could not verify the `chargekit` SDK's actual interface (e.g., whether it exposes a native refund method, idempotency keys, etc.) — it isn't vendored in this repo, so I only know it from how it's called (`.charge(tenant=..., amount=..., currency=...)`, `.create_customer(...)`, `errors.ChargeDeclined`/`ProviderUnavailable`). Anything about how a real `refund()` call should be shaped against the actual SDK would need the SDK's docs or source, which are outside this repository.
- I did not find any CI configuration, pre-commit hook, or lint rule in `medibill` enforcing the gateway boundary, but I only checked for the obvious locations (`.github/`, `scripts/`); I can't rule out enforcement living somewhere outside this project entirely (e.g., an org-wide linter not present in this checkout).
- Scope: I'm treating `medibill` as the sole intended target because it's the only project in this repository with a `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and a payment integration — this was confirmed by two repo-wide searches, not assumed.

The doc names one exception; there are actually two additional, unlisted ones, and the "rule" itself isn't followed by any live code path.
- **Invoice API response** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:11-12`): claims the response contains *exactly* `id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status`. The actual `InvoiceOut` dataclass (`medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py:16-22`) has a sixth field, `external_ref`, populated from the provider's charge result and returned by `charge_invoice()` (`invoices.py:17-18`). This field was added in commit `c23da88` (MEDB-214, 2026-06-18), backed by `medibill/backend/migrations/0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql`, without any change to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in that commit (confirmed via `git show --stat c23da88`, which touches `invoices.py`, `webhooks.py`, `models/invoice.py`, and the migration file, but not `docs/`). The doc's "exactly" claim was accurate against the original schema (`migrations/0001_step1.sql`, 5 columns) but has been wrong since MEDB-214.

**3. What should happen to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when.**

Per the skill's standard — drift is a bug, and the contract must be corrected in the same commit as the code that invalidates it — this is a **stop-and-surface moment before writing any refund code**, not a cleanup to defer. Concretely:

- The doc needs to be corrected now, as its own explicitly-called-out change, before or atomically with the refund feature — not silently folded into the refund PR's diff. Refund support is exactly the kind of payment-integration change this doc governs, so building it on top of a contract that's already been false for weeks (since 2026-06-18) compounds the same failure a third time.
- For the payment-integration section, the team needs to make an explicit choice rather than have it improvised: either (a) fix `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` to route through `gateway.py`, restoring the truth of the "every call goes through gateway.py" rule, or (b) if there's a real reason those two need direct access, update the doc to name them as accepted exceptions. Given the skill's guidance that "an existing exception is not a license to add a new one elsewhere without the same scrutiny the original one presumably got," and that `gateway.py` is currently *unused* (not a case of one or two deliberate carve-outs alongside heavy legitimate use), option (a) looks like the right default — but that's a call for whoever owns this code, not one I should make unilaterally while read-only.
- The invoice API section needs `external_ref` added to the field list (or the field list needs to be re-derived from `InvoiceOut` directly — see Q4's logic, which applies here too).
- Whatever refund support adds — a new `gateway.refund()` call site, any new response field, any new webhook type — must land with its own doc update in the same commit, called out in the commit/PR summary, per the skill's core rule.

**4. The webhook event-type count claim.**

`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:16-17` states: *"The integration handles 4 webhook event types: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened."* The actual handler, `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py:7-20`, branches on **5** types: `payment_succeeded`, `payment_failed`, `payment_pending`, `refund_created`, `dispute_opened`. `payment_pending` was added in commit `c23da88` (MEDB-214) — the same commit that added `external_ref` — again with no accompanying change to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`.

This is precisely the failure mode the skill's "Don't cite a number: read the source" guidance warns about: a hardcoded count in prose that was correct when written and has since silently gone stale. What I'd change about how it's stated: drop the numeral ("4 webhook event types") and either list the types without asserting a total, or better, point at `app/api/webhooks.py`'s dispatch as the source of truth rather than duplicating the list in prose at all. A refund feature is likely to add at least one more event type (e.g., a `refund_failed`/`refund_succeeded` case), so a restated fixed count would just go stale a second time; a doc that names the file instead of a number can't drift the same way.
sections.confidence and gaps:
High confidence, directly verified by reading the code and git history:
- The gateway-bypass finding (Q1/Q2): confirmed both by reading `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` line-by-line and by a repo-wide grep showing zero call sites for `gateway.charge`/`gateway.create_customer`. This is as solid as read-only inspection gets.
- The `external_ref` field and `payment_pending` gaps (Q2/Q4): confirmed by direct code inspection, cross-checked against the migration that added the column and the exact commit diff (`git show c23da88`) that added both changes without touching `docs/`.
- No existing refund-issuing code: confirmed by a repo-wide case-insensitive grep for "refund," which only found the inbound webhook branch, nothing that calls out to the provider.

Lower confidence / judgment calls, not facts:
- My Q3 recommendation to fix `invoices.py`/`dunning.py` to route through `gateway.py` (rather than document them as accepted exceptions) is my read of the skill's stated preference, not something the repository itself dictates — I flagged it as a decision for the code owner rather than asserting it as the only correct answer, per the skill's own instruction to treat an ambiguous contract as a stop-and-surface moment rather than something to improvise past.
- I could not verify the `chargekit` SDK's actual interface (e.g., whether it exposes a native refund method, idempotency keys, etc.) — it isn't vendored in this repo, so I only know it from how it's called (`.charge(tenant=..., amount=..., currency=...)`, `.create_customer(...)`, `errors.ChargeDeclined`/`ProviderUnavailable`). Anything about how a real `refund()` call should be shaped against the actual SDK would need the SDK's docs or source, which are outside this repository.
- I did not find any CI configuration, pre-commit hook, or lint rule in `medibill` enforcing the gateway boundary, but I only checked for the obvious locations (`.github/`, `scripts/`); I can't rule out enforcement living somewhere outside this project entirely (e.g., an org-wide linter not present in this checkout).
- Scope: I'm treating `medibill` as the sole intended target because it's the only project in this repository with a `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and a payment integration — this was confirmed by two repo-wide searches, not assumed.
sections.next steps:
If I were allowed to make changes, in this order:

1. Raise the two `gateway.py` bypasses (`invoices.py`, `dunning.py`) and the two silent doc-vs-code gaps (`external_ref`, `payment_pending`) with whoever owns `medibill` *before* starting refund work, since the refund feature both depends on and would extend the payment-integration contract.
2. Get an explicit decision on `invoices.py`/`dunning.py`: refactor them to call `gateway.charge()` (making `gateway.py` actually load-bearing), or formally document them as exceptions — then make the code and `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` agree, in one commit.
3. Update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`'s invoice-API section to include `external_ref`, and its webhooks section to name `payment_pending` and drop the hardcoded count.
4. Add `refund()` to `gateway.py` (mirroring `charge()`/`create_customer()`), a route that calls only that accessor, and — if refunds arrive as webhooks too — extend `webhooks.py`'s dispatch, all landing together with the corresponding `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` update in the same commit.
5. Replace the placeholder in `tests/test_webhooks.py:1-2` with real tests asserting the actual set of handled event types, so this specific drift (doc says N, code does N+1) is caught mechanically next time rather than by manual doc review.
6. Consider a small grep-based check (the skill gives the exact pattern) run in CI: flag any `import chargekit` outside `app/billing/gateway.py` and `app/billing/errors.py`, so a third silent bypass can't reappear. (`medibill` currently has no CI workflow and no such guard script, unlike at least one sibling project in this repo that does have an equivalent check.)
sections.plan: The task asked me to review, read-only, how the backend that will gain refund support currently integrates with its payment provider, and to check whether `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` still matches that code. I first read the mandated skill (`architecture-contracts-as-law`) to understand the standard I should hold the doc to: contract docs are meant to be merge-blocking law, updated in the same commit as any code that changes what they govern, with drift treated as a bug. I then located the target project (this repo contains many unrelated small apps; only `medibill` has a `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and a payment integration), read every file touching the payment provider, cross-checked each of `ARCHITECTURE.md`'s three claims (gateway boundary, invoice API shape, webhook event types) against the actual code, and used git history to see exactly when and how the doc and code diverged.
